curator: auto (generate_day.py — reals verified verbatim vs source + cross-validated)

quotes.text: May the Force be with you.
quotes.speaker: Star Wars (1977)
quotes.context: Said during the Rebel briefing before the assault on the Death Star (and by Han to Luke).

quotes.text: There's something about this that's so black, it's like how much more black could this be? And the answer is none. None more black.
quotes.speaker: This Is Spinal Tap (1984)
quotes.context: Nigel Tufnel (Christopher Guest) shows off a new album cover; his deadpan musing on its blackness.

quotes.text: You can set your watch by how wrong he is.
quotes.speaker: His Girl Friday (1940)
quotes.context: Hildy Johnson venting about her editor Walter Burns to a colleague
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the rapid-fire, wisecracking exasperation is exactly the register Hildy uses when talking about Walter, and the clock metaphor suits the film's newsroom obsession with deadlines. The giveaway is it's a touch too polished; Hawksian screwball dialogue is usually messier and more overlapping.

quotes.text: Don't thank me yet. I haven't decided if I like you.
quotes.speaker: Midnight Cowboy (1969)
quotes.context: Ratso Rizzo to Joe Buck after steering him away from a scam
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Ratso's prickly, self-protective generosity toward Joe is the emotional core of the film, and this captures his voice well. The tell is it's slightly too pithy; Hoffman's Ratso tends to ramble and mutter rather than land clean one-liners.

quotes.text: You want me to feel sorry for you? Stand in line.
quotes.speaker: All About Eve (1950)
quotes.context: Margo Channing dismissing a rival's complaint backstage
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Margo's sharp, theatrical cruelty makes this feel right at home, and the rhythm echoes her real dialogue. The subtle tell is that 'stand in line' is a touch too breezy; her actual put-downs tend to be longer and more theatrical.

quotes.text: Every man in that room wanted to be him, and he knew it, which was exactly the problem.
quotes.speaker: Amadeus (1984)
quotes.context: Salieri narrating about Mozart's entrance at a formal gathering
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Salieri's envious, confessional narration runs throughout the film in exactly this reflective register. The giveaway is the line is a fraction too neat and analytical; his real narration tends to be more emotionally raw and self-lacerating.
